document.title: Zoo Project JSON Format
document.purpose: Describe the JSON format used to represent a single project in Zoo.
author: Lion Kimbro
Wing-Cat (ChatGPT)

overview.basic: Each Zoo project is stored as a single JSON file. The file represents one project.
overview.identity: Each project has a globally unique identifier stored in the 'id' field. This is the canonical identity.
overview.philosophy: The format is minimal and extensible. Only a small set of fields are defined. Additional fields may be added as needed.

file-structure.location: Each project is stored in its own file under zoo/projects/.
file-structure.filename: The filename is typically the project's GUID, for example: <id>.json.

id.description: Globally unique identifier for the project (typically a GUID). This is the canonical identity of the project.

tag.description: Optional tag URI providing a human-meaningful identity for the project. May be added later.

title.description: Short human-readable name of the project.

created.description: Date the project was created, in YYYY-MM-DD format.

tags.description: Loose keywords associated with the project. Used for search and grouping.

description.description: Longer description of the project, providing context and meaning.

hook.description: A short, activating phrase that helps re-enter the project. Often more evocative than the description.

example-project.title: Documenting & Developing My Data Formats
example-project.description: Ongoing effort to document and develop reusable data formats for shared use.
example-project.hook: Developing a shared language of data formats.

conventions: Use kebab-case or simple lowercase words for keys.
Do not require all optional fields.
Prefer adding fields over modifying existing ones.
Do not store filesystem paths or locations in this file.
Treat this file as the conceptual identity of the project, not its implementation.
notes: The 'id' field is the only required field and must never change.
The 'tag' field may be added later when the project becomes well-defined.
This format intentionally avoids defining project state (such as active or inactive). State belongs to other systems such as the Temple of Focus.
Additional fields may be introduced as the system evolves.
